37: agapeseis kyrion ton theon sou en hole te kardia sou kai en hole te psyche sou kai en hole te dianoia sou — you shall love the Lord your God with all your heart and all your soul and all your mind — the Shema (Deut 6:5 LXX) adds dianoia (mind, understanding) to the MT's heart-soul-strength, creating a triad that covers the affective, volitional, and cognitive dimensions of the whole person. The totality-markers (holos = whole, in all three cases) indicate that the love commanded is total — leaving no part of the person divided or uncommitted.
44: eipen kyrios to kyrio mou — the LORD said to my Lord — the LXX renders Ps 110:1's YHWH ladon (YHWH to my Adonai); Jesus's argument turns on David calling his own descendant Lord (kyrios), which would be unseemly if the son were merely human. The Davidic messianic expectation assumed the son-of-David would be David's inferior; Jesus uses Ps 110:1 to demonstrate that the Messiah is simultaneously son and superior — a paradox that points to the divine sonship.
1: apokritheis ho Iēsous palin eipen — Jesus answering again spoke — Apokritheis (answering) even without a preceding question is a Semitism inherited from LXX (wayyaʿan); it introduces a new discourse. Palin (again) links this parable directly to the preceding sequence.
2: hōmoiōthē hē basileia tōn ouranōn anthrōpō̧ basilei — the kingdom of heaven was likened to a king — The Wedding Banquet parable (cf. Luke 14:15-24 — a different version). Hostis epoiēsen gamous tō̧ huiō̧ autou (who made a wedding feast for his son) — gamos (wedding, wedding feast) is the central image; the singular and plural forms both occur in vv. 2-12.
3: apesteilen tous doulous autou kalesai tous keklēmenous — he sent his servants to call those who had been invited — Tous keklēmenous (the having-been-called, perfect passive participle) — those pre-invited; the initial invitation is assumed. Kai ouk ēthelon elthein (and they were not willing to come) — the imperfect ēthelon = ongoing unwillingness, sustained refusal.
4: ta sitista mou etoimasin — my fatted animals are prepared — Sitista (fatted animals, grain-fed cattle) indicates a lavish feast. Panta hetoima (all things are ready) — the perfect hetoima (in a state of readiness) underscores that the only remaining action is the guests’ arrival; they have no legitimate excuse.
5: amelēsantes de apēlthon — having been careless/indifferent they went off — Ameleō (to be unconcerned, neglect) is the key word; the refusal is not hostile but indifferent — ho men eis ton idion agron (one to his own field), ho de eis tēn emporian autou (another to his business). Economic preoccupation displaces the invitation.
6: hoi de loipoi kratēsantes tous doulous autou hybrizan kai apekteinan — the rest seizing his servants insulted and killed them — Hybrizō (to insult, mistreat, outrage) connotes deliberate humiliation beyond mere violence. The escalation from indifference (v.5) to murder (v.6) parallels the Wicked Tenants sequence, reinforcing the allegory of Israel’s rejection of prophets.
7: orgistheis de ho basileus apesteilen ta strateumata autou — the king having become angry sent his armies — Strateumata (armies, troops) — unusual for a wedding-feast context; the allegorical dimension is transparent. Eneprēsen tēn polin ekeinēn (burned that city) is widely read as a reference to the destruction of Jerusalem in AD 70 (the aorist describing a future-from-Jesus’-perspective event).
8: ho men gamos hetoimos estin — the wedding feast is ready — The present estin maintains the feast’s continued readiness despite the refusals. Hoi de keklēmenoi ouk ēsan axioi (those who were called were not worthy) — axios (worthy, deserving) — the judgment is on fitness, not on God’s desire to include them.
9: epi tas diexodous tōn hodōn — to the crossings of the roads — Diexodos (thoroughfare, where roads cross and split, outer edges) = the main intersections and exits of the city, where the most diverse traffic would be found. Hosous ean heurēte kalesate eis tous gamous (whoever you find call to the wedding) — the universality of the new invitation.
10: synēgagon pantas hous heuron ponērous te kai agathous — they gathered all they found, evil and good alike — Ponērous te kai agathous (both evil and good) — the mission to the crossroads gathers without discrimination; the mixed community prefigures the tares-among-wheat dynamic (cf. 13:24-30). Eplēsthē ho nymphōn anakeimenōn (the wedding hall was filled with those reclining) — anakeimenōn (reclining at table) is the posture of banquet guests.
11: eiselthen de ho basileus theasasthai tous anakeimenous — the king came in to see those reclining — Theasasthai (to look at carefully, behold) aorist infinitive of theaomai; a formal inspection. Eidon ekei anthrōpon ouk endedymenon endyma gamou (he saw there a man not clothed with a wedding garment) — endyma gamou (wedding garment) — the absence is the focus, not just improper dress.
12: hetaire pōs eisēlthes hōde mē echōn endyma gamou — friend, how did you enter here not having a wedding garment? — Hetaire (companion, friend — distancing form; cf. 20:13; 26:50) used three times in Matthew in contexts of gentle rebuke. Ho de ephimōthē (he was silenced) — phimoō (to muzzle, silence) aorist passive; the man has no answer, indicating his guilt is recognized even by himself.
13: dēsantes autou podas kai cheiras ekballete auton — bind his feet and hands and cast him out — Dēsantes (having bound) aorist participle; the binding renders the man helpless. Eis to skotos to exōteron (into the outer darkness) with ho klauthmos kai ho brygmos tōn odontōn (the weeping and gnashing of teeth) = the standard Matthean formula for eschatological judgment (8:12; 13:42,50; 24:51; 25:30).
14: polloi gar eisin klētoi oligoi de eklektoi — for many are called but few are chosen — Klētoi (called, invited) and eklektoi (chosen, selected — from eklegō = to choose out) are distinct: the call is universal, the election is particular. This serves as the parable’s theological summary and anticipates the judgment scene of 25:31-46.
15: poreutheies synebouleusanto hopōs auton pagideuōsin en logō̧ — having departed they plotted to trap him in a word — Pagideuō (to trap, ensnare — from pagis = snare) is a hunting metaphor; the Pharisees aim to catch Jesus in a statement that either alienates the crowd or invites Roman action. En logō̧ (in a word/statement) = by means of his own words.
16: alēthēs ei kai tēn hodon tou theou en alētheia didaskeis — you are truthful and teach the way of God in truth — The flattery (vv. 16b) is transparent but formally correct; it sets up the question as if they sincerely sought Jesus’ unbiased opinion. Ou melei soi peri oudenos (you care about no one) = you are impartial, immune to social pressure — the very quality they hope to exploit.
17: exestin dounai kēnson Kaisari ē ou — is it lawful to pay tribute to Caesar or not? — Kēnsos (census tax, poll tax — from Latin census) was the annual per-head tax paid to Rome, deeply resented. The dilemma: yes = betrayal of Jewish national sentiment; no = sedition against Rome. Either answer seemed to destroy Jesus.
18: gnous de ho Iēsous tēn ponērian autōn — Jesus knowing their wickedness — Gnous (aorist participle of ginōskō) = having known (supernatural insight). Tēn ponērian (their wickedness, evil intent) — Matthew uses the strong moral term ponēria. Ti me peiraxete hypokritai (why do you test me, hypocrites?) — hypokritēs (stage-actor, one who plays a part) is Jesus’ sharpest address for religious performance.
19: epideíxaté moi to nomisma tou kēnsou — show me the tribute coin — Nomisma (coin in common use, currency — from nomos = law/custom) = the standard coin; specifically a denarius bearing Caesar’s image. Jesus’ request for the coin shifts the burden to them — they produce it, implying their participation in the Roman economy.
20: tinos hē eikōn hautē kai hē epigraphē — whose image and inscription is this? — Eikōn (image, likeness) and epigraphē (inscription, superscription) together establish identity and ownership. The question invites them to articulate the coin’s nature before Jesus draws the theological conclusion.
21: apodote oun ta Kaisaros Kaisari kai ta tou theou tō̧ theō̧ — give back therefore the things of Caesar to Caesar and the things of God to God — Apodidōmi (to give back, render) rather than didōmi (to give) implies returning what is already owed — obligation, not gift. The answer is neither capitulation to Rome nor seditious refusal; it distinguishes two orders of obligation without subordinating the divine to the imperial. Ta tou theou tō̧ theō̧ — the humanity who bears God’s image (eikōn, Gen 1:26-27) belongs to God.
22: thaumasantes aphēkan auton kai apēlthon — marveling they left him and went away — Thaumazō (to marvel, be amazed) without the further step of faith recurs in Matthew as a response that falls short of discipleship. The adversaries are silenced but not converted.
23: Saddoukaioi hoi legontes mē einai anastasin — Sadducees who say there is no resurrection — Matthew’s parenthetical gloss identifies the Sadducees by their distinctive doctrinal position: rejection of bodily resurrection (held only Torah as authoritative, not the Prophets or Writings where resurrection texts appear). Epērōtēsan auton (questioned him) — aorist, a single formal question.
24: Mōysēs eipen ean tis apothanē mē echōn tekna — Moses said if someone dies not having children — The Sadducees cite Deut 25:5-10 (Levirate marriage law: epigambrieuein = to marry the brother’s widow). Anastēsei sperma tō̧ adelphō̧ autou (will raise up offspring for his brother) — a technical legal obligation to preserve the deceased brother’s lineage.
25: ēsan de par' hēmin hepta adelphoi — there were among us seven brothers — The constructed scenario escalates to absurdity (seven levirate marriages) precisely to make the resurrection afterlife appear logically incoherent. Aphēken tēn gynaika autou tō̧ adelphō̧ autou (left his wife to his brother) — standard levirate fulfillment vocabulary.
26: homoiōs kai ho deuteros kai ho tritos heōs tōn hepta — likewise the second and the third through the seven — The rapid enumeration (homoiōs = likewise, in the same way) through all seven compresses the scenario to reveal its reductio ad absurdum intent.
27: hysteron de pantōn apethanen hē gynē — last of all the woman also died — The final death closes the hypothetical chain. Hysteron... pantōn (last of all) places the woman’s death as the culminating event that makes the resurrection question unavoidable.
28: en tȩ̄ anastásei oun tinos tōn hepta estai gynē — in the resurrection therefore whose wife of the seven will she be? — The question assumes resurrection must replicate earthly social structures (marriage and ownership) — the very assumption Jesus will demolish. Pantes gar eschon autēn (for all had her) — the final phrase is designed for maximum logical shock.
29: planasthe mē eidotes tas graphas mēde tēn dynamin tou theou — you are deceived not knowing the scriptures nor the power of God — Planasthe (present passive: you are being led astray, deceived) is a strong charge; the Sadducees’ error stems from two ignorances: tas graphas (the scriptures) and tēn dynamin tou theou (the power of God). Both are required to understand resurrection.
30: en tȩ̄ anastásei oute gamousin oute gamizontai — in the resurrection they neither marry nor are given in marriage — Gameō (to marry, active: the man’s act) and gamizō (to give in marriage, the father’s act of betrothal) distinguish the two parties in Jewish marriage customs. Resurrection life transcends the social institution entirely. All' hōs angeloi en tō̧ ouranō̧ eisin (but as angels in heaven they are) — the comparison is to angelic existence, not to dissolution of personhood.
31: peri de tēs anastaseōs tōn nekrōn ouk anegnōte — concerning the resurrection of the dead have you not read? — Jesus now defeats the Sadducees on their own canonical ground: the Torah itself (not the Prophets). To rhēthen hymin hypo tou theou (what was spoken to you by God) — direct divine speech, not merely human author.
32: egō eimi ho theos Abraam kai ho theos Isaak kai ho theos Iakōb — I am the God of Abraham and the God of Isaac and the God of Jacob — Ex 3:6 LXX cited with precision. The present tense eimi (I am) is the argument: God speaks of himself as the God of Abraham in the present, not the past — therefore Abraham exists presently in relationship with God. Ouk estin ho theos nekrōn alla zōntōn (he is not God of the dead but of the living) — the logical inference: if God is Abraham’s God presently, Abraham must be living.
33: kai akousantes hoi ochloi exeplēssonto epi tȩ̄ didachȩ̄ autou — the crowds hearing were astonished at his teaching — Ekplēssō (to strike out of one’s senses, astound) perfect passive — the astonishment is complete and ongoing. Epi tȩ̄ didachȩ̄ autou (at his teaching) — it is the content and method of the argument, not merely a miracle, that produces this response.
34: hoi de Pharisaioi akousantes hoti ephimōsen tous Saddoukiaous — the Pharisees having heard that he had silenced the Sadducees — Phimoō (to muzzle, silence) aorist: Jesus’ defeat of the Sadducees satisfies the Pharisees doctrinally but renews their own challenge. Synēchthēsan epi to auto (were gathered together) — a formal assembly to coordinate the next question.
35: nomikos peirazōn auton — a lawyer testing him — Nomikos (lawyer, legal expert in Torah — not Roman law) appears only here in Matthew; Mark 12:28 describes him more sympathetically. Peirazōn (testing, present participle) marks adversarial intent, though the question itself is a legitimate and important one in rabbinic discussion.
36: poia entolē megalē en tō̧ nomō̧ — which commandment is great in the law? — The question about the greatest commandment (megalē entolē) was a live rabbinic debate; various sages offered summaries. The lawyer seeks either to catch Jesus in a controversial ranking or to hear his answer for debate purposes.
38: hautē estin hē megalē kai prōtē entolē — this is the great and first commandment — Jesus identifies Deut 6:5 (the Shema continuation) as megalē kai prōtē (great and first). En holȩ̄ tȩ̄ kardia̧ sou kai en holȩ̄ tȩ̄ psychȩ̄ sou kai en holȩ̄ tȩ̄ dianoia̧ sou — Matthew’s version adds dianoia (mind/understanding) to the Deut 6:5 triad of heart/soul/strength, following the LXX variant.
39: deutera de homoia autȩ̄ — and a second is like it — Homoia (like, similar) — Jesus’ addition of the second commandment (Lev 19:18: agapēseis ton plēsion sou hōs seauton) was not asked; he volunteers it as inseparably paired with the first. The neighbor-love command is not subordinate but homoia = of the same kind and order.
40: en tautais tais dysin entolais holos ho nomos krematai kai hoi prophētai — on these two commandments hang all the law and the prophets — Krematai (hangs, depends — from kremannymi) is a striking metaphor: the entire Torah and prophetic corpus hangs from these two love-commands as weights hang from a beam. This is the Matthean summary of scriptural ethics (cf. 7:12).
41: synēgmenōn de tōn Pharisaiōn — the Pharisees being gathered together — Jesus now takes the initiative; having answered all challenges he poses the decisive Christological question. Epērōtēsen autous ho Iēsous (Jesus questioned them) — the reversal: the interrogated becomes the interrogator.
42: tinos huios estin ho christos — whose son is the Christ? — The fundamental question of identity: whose descendant (huios) is the Messiah? Their answer tou Dauid (of David) is correct but insufficient; Jesus will show it is incomplete. The question anticipates 22:43-45.
43: pōs oun Dauid en pneumati kalei auton kyrion — how then does David in the Spirit call him Lord? — En pneumati (in the Spirit) = under divine inspiration, the standard understanding of prophetic authorship (cf. 2 Sam 23:2; 2 Tim 3:16). David’s psalm-composition is Spirit-inspired, giving his words in Ps 110 the character of divine oracle.
45: ei oun Dauid kalei auton kyrion — if therefore David calls him Lord — The conditional is straightforward: if David (as author) called this figure kyrios, and the Messiah is David’s descendant, a contradiction appears under ordinary kinship logic. Pōs huios autou estin (how is he his son?) — the question forces recognition that the Messiah transcends human genealogical categories; he is simultaneously David’s son and David’s Lord.
46: kai oudeis edynato apokrithēnai autō̧ logon — and no one was able to answer him a word — Apokrithēnai logon (to answer a word) = to give any reply at all. The silencing of all challengers is total and deliberate: the chief priests, Herodians, Sadducees, and Pharisees have all failed. Oude etólmēsen tis (nor did anyone dare) — tolmaō (to dare, have courage) — even the attempt at further question was abandoned.
